title:
Will the first of a kind BWRX-300 SMR cost CAD 7.2 billion in overnight construction cost?

The Tenessee Valley Authority's latest integrated resource plan estimates 'light water SMR' (AKA BWRX-300)… | Chris Keefer MD | 158 comments
text:
Will the first of a kind BWRX-300 SMR cost CAD 7.2 billion in overnight construction cost? The Tenessee Valley Authority's latest integrated resource plan estimates 'light water SMR' (AKA BWRX-300) first-of-a-kind overnight construction cost at an eye-watering USD $17,949/kW (2024$) for a 2033 completion. That is CAD $24,297/kW or roughly CAD 7.2 billion for the FOAK BWRX-300. This shocking factoid from the Tenessee Valley Authority's most recent Integrated Resource Planning document has been getting shockingly little attention and needs broader discussion within the industry. While this is very preliminary and no cost estimate is perfect, TVA should have a significant amount of insight given its participation in the GE Hitachi Nuclear Energy BWRX-300 design development. It's been a bad week for SMRs. The DOE's published its most recent "Updated Pathway to Advanced Nuclear Commercial Lift Off" report this week and it is quite frank in recognizing the diseconomies of Small Reactors. As previously noted on Decouple Media, civil works are the number one driver of nuclear construction cost, and in Ontario we have chosen for our SMR experiment a reactor with an embedded vessel resulting in proportionately larger and more complex civil works than our large reactor alternatives. All this is occuring in the context of sky-rocketing power demand forecasts from economic growth let alone the coming AI boom. We have in Ontario Power Generation's Darlington B a precious site licensed for 4800 MW and we risk squandering it for a measly 1200 MW which just makes up for the loss of Unit 1&4 at Pickering A. I struggle to see how SMRs in Southern Ontario with its booming demand growth make sense, particularly if the TVA's eye watering price tag is anywhere near accurate. Stephen Lecce TVA 2025 Draft Integrated Resource Plan https://lnkd.in/g7FgXxjK DOE Updated Pathway to Advanced Nuclear Commercial Lift Off https://lnkd.in/g9JRZj-q Decouple Media: The big problem with SMRs https://lnkd.in/gyPfBgwY
You do realize that this is a FOAK price estimate, right? FOAK!? Just like the first EPR’s and CANDU’s and BWR’s were? Those cost are just sunk costs now. Right?
In an IRP, they're going to put out a very conservative number. Under-promise, over-deliver.
Unpopular opinion: Darlington B should be built as God (and Ontario Hydro) intended, add 4x Monark's (evolution of the Darlington design) to the site, keep it 100% Canadian and CANDU and fill-out that site license. I'm still not sold on building an American design that requires imported enriched fuel in Canada, when we developed our own reactor technology specifically to avoid that in the first place.
We need SMRs for niche applications and island grids, but a large grid like TVA and Ontario don’t need them for bulk electricity generation. Let a place like Alberta pioneer them. Build CANDU & AP1000 instead
Light water SMR may very well be a 6 module NuScale plant using the 50 MW VOYGR design. It would make no sense for OPG to select such an offer after rejecting the CANDU proposal with a price in the same ballpark as being much too expensive. GE-Hitachi hopes to hit a sweet spot between large reactors marred by delays and costs overruns and too small SMRs having lost the economy of vertical scale. Cost estimates are hard to come by and that could be a good thing given how even small budget overruns are a talking point for the antinuclear lobby. The good thing is the FOAK are already being built so in a few short years only the NOAK will matter. It's worrying CANDU couldn't make a competitive offer back then. India is able to deliver HWPR at rock-bottom prices so the technology itself doesn't seem to be the main culprit.
DOE knew long ago about SMRs diseconomies of scale, & lack of full factory build, it is why DOD abandoned SMRs when they didn't get what DOD/DOE asked for. It is about time that DOE admitted that they were only trying to do SMRs for military purposes and that there was no chance that it was going to be economically competitive commercial, and especially admitting that no one designing SMRs has designed them to be neither small nor modular with full factory assembly of the reactor and of the power conversion unit so that they can be shipped down the road fully assembled and fueled, as DOD requested.
Nuclear is the most expensive way to make electricity, now and in all future.
Just 2 days ago - if you think this is a 'paper reactor', you're nuts: "...We’re making tremendous strides on the standard design of the BWRX-300 Small Modular Reactor........we remain on track to deliver North America’s first SMR within this decade.....Small Modular Reactors are a game-changing technology that will be essential to ensure that nuclear remains a cornerstone of the energy mix, providing dispatchable and sustainable power to meet growing electricity demand..." https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mavi-zingoni_nuclear-smallmodularreactor-smr-activity-7250487051958059008-1bfK?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop
Thanks Chris, does the IRP get into what the design basis assumptions were for the Gen IV SMR column?
Really appreciate the informative article. I can’t help but think though that OPG understands the risks, but is proceeding due to the cost of maintenance to power lines and the loss of power per distance of travel across such a vast land mass. I would love for Monarks to be built everywhere, but I can also understand the desire for SMR’s, especially in Canada.
